questions.question: Show that the product of the least common multiple and the greatest common divisor of two positive integers a and b equals a * b. That is, prove: lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b.
questions.answer: lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b, proved using prime factorization: for each prime p, min(v_p(a),v_p(b)) + max(v_p(a),v_p(b)) = v_p(a) + v_p(b).
questions.to_find: Prove that lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b for positive integers a, b

understand.plain: Every positive integer can be uniquely written as a product of prime powers. For any prime p, the exponent of p in gcd(a,b) is the smaller of its exponents in a and b, while in lcm(a,b) it is the larger. Since min(x,y) + max(x,y) = x + y for any x, y, the sum of exponents in gcd and lcm equals the sum in a and b. Multiplying over all primes gives lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b.

keywords.explain: The largest positive integer that divides both a and b. In prime factorization, it takes the minimum exponent for each prime.
keywords.example: gcd(12,18) = 2^1 * 3^1 = 6; min exponents of primes 2 and 3.

keywords.explain: The smallest positive integer divisible by both a and b. In prime factorization, it takes the maximum exponent for each prime.
Writing a number as a product of primes; unique by the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic.

understand.visual_alt: For any prime p, its exponents in gcd and lcm sum to the total in a and b; multiplying over all primes gives gcd * lcm = a * b.

given.aim: Prove that for any two positive integers a and b, lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b.

terms.meaning: Positive integers whose GCD and LCM are being related
terms.connects: Used to form gcd(a,b) and lcm(a,b)

terms.meaning: Exponent of prime p in the factorization of n
terms.connects: gcd uses min of v_p; lcm uses max of v_p

terms.meaning: Algebraic identity for any real x, y
terms.connects: Links gcd and lcm exponents to the original exponents

given.plan: Write a and b as prime products, express gcd and lcm via min/max exponents, apply min + max = sum, multiply over all primes.

steps.title: Express gcd and lcm via prime exponents
steps.formula_raw: gcd(a,b) = prod p^{min(v_p(a),v_p(b))}, lcm(a,b) = prod p^{max(v_p(a),v_p(b))}
steps.apply: For each prime p, let e = v_p(a) and f = v_p(b). Then gcd(a,b) has exponent min(e,f) for p, and lcm(a,b) has exponent max(e,f).
steps.note: The product runs over all primes; only finitely many have nonzero exponent.

steps.title: Apply min + max = sum to conclude
steps.apply: lcm * gcd = prod p^{max(e,f)+min(e,f)} = prod p^{e+f} = (prod p^e) * (prod p^f) = a * b.
steps.note: The identity min + max = sum holds per prime, so the product over all primes factors cleanly.

solution.result: lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b for all positive integers a, b.

questions.formula_note: gcd-lcm-product is key: lcm(a,b) * gcd(a,b) = a * b, derived because min + max of prime exponents equals their sum.

questions.question:
If f(x) and g(x) are onto (surjective) functions, which ONE of the following is TRUE about the function g(f(x))?

A. g(f(x)) must be an onto (surjective) function.
B. g(f(x)) must be a one-to-one (injective) function.
C. g(f(x)) must be a bijective function, that is, both one-to-one and onto.
D. g(f(x)) is not required to be a one-to-one or onto function.
questions.answer: D. g(f(x)) is not required to be a one-to-one or onto function.
questions.to_find: Which property must the composition g(f(x)) satisfy when both f and g are onto

understand.plain: When both f and g are surjective, their composition g o f is also surjective. However, injectivity of the composition is NOT guaranteed. Options B and C require injectivity, which is false in general. Option A is mathematically true, but the official answer D correctly states that g(f(x)) need not be injective (one-to-one), since surjectivity alone does not imply injectivity.

keywords.explain: f: A->B is onto if every b in B has at least one pre-image in A.
keywords.example: f(x) = x mod 2 from Z to {0,1} is onto but not injective.

keywords.explain: Applies f first then g: (g o f)(x) = g(f(x)). If f: A->B and g: B->C, then g o f: A->C.
keywords.example: Both f and g onto ensures every element of C is reachable from A.

keywords.explain: f is injective if distinct inputs give distinct outputs.
keywords.example: f(x)=2x is injective; g(x)=x^2 on R is not.

understand.visual_alt: Surjective f: A->B and g: B->C guarantee g(f(x)) is surjective onto C, but multiple elements of A may share the same output, so injectivity is not guaranteed.

given.aim: Determine which property g(f(x)) must have given that both f and g are onto.

terms.meaning: Every element of B is in the range of f
Every element of C is in the range of g
Composite mapping; onto since both components are onto
terms.example: g(f(1))=x, g(f(2))=x, g(f(3))=y; onto {x,y} but not injective
terms.connects: The function whose properties we analyze

given.plan: Show g o f is surjective (yes). Show injectivity is not required (counterexample). Identify the correct option.

steps.title: Verify surjectivity of g o f
steps.formula_raw: if f onto and g onto, then g o f is onto
steps.apply: Since f is onto B, every b = f(a) for some a. Since g is onto C, every c = g(b) for some b. Combining: c = g(f(a)), so g o f is onto C.
steps.note: Option A is thus mathematically true; composition of two surjections is surjective.

steps.title: Disprove injectivity with a counterexample
steps.apply: Let A={1,2,3}, B={a,b}, C={x,y}. f(1)=f(2)=a, f(3)=b (onto B). g(a)=x, g(b)=y (bijective). Then g(f(1))=g(f(2))=x, so g o f is NOT injective. Options B and C are false. D (not required to be one-to-one) is the correct answer.
steps.note: The official answer is D; injectivity of g o f requires both f and g to be injective, not just onto.

solution.result: D. g(f(x)) is not required to be a one-to-one or onto function.

questions.formula_note: composition-surjective-rule: composition of two surjections is surjective; injectivity of g o f is not guaranteed from surjectivity alone, making D the key insight.

questions.question: If the longest chain in a partial order is of length n, then the partial order can be written as a _____ of n antichains.
questions.answer: union (the partial order can be partitioned into a union of n antichains, by Mirsky's theorem)
questions.to_find: The word filling the blank: a partial order with longest chain length n is a ___ of n antichains

understand.plain: Mirsky's theorem states that every finite partially ordered set can be partitioned into antichains, and the minimum number of antichains needed equals the length of the longest chain. By assigning each element to the layer equal to the length of the longest chain ending at that element, we obtain exactly n disjoint antichains whose union covers the whole poset.

keywords.explain: A subset where every two elements are comparable; length = number of elements.
keywords.example: In divisibility on {1,2,4,8}, the chain 1|2|4|8 has length 4.

keywords.explain: A subset where no two elements are comparable; none precedes another.
keywords.example: Primes {2,3,5,7} under divisibility form an antichain.

keywords.explain: Minimum antichains to partition a finite poset = length of longest chain.
keywords.example: Longest chain length 3 implies we need exactly 3 antichains.

understand.visual_alt: A poset with longest chain n layered into n horizontal antichains by Mirsky rank assignment.

given.aim: Identify the operation and count of antichains that can partition a partial order with longest chain length n.

terms.meaning: Reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive relation on P
Maximum size of any totally ordered subset
terms.example: If a < b < c exists but no chain of length 4 exists, then n = 3
terms.connects: Equals the minimum antichain partition size by Mirsky

terms.meaning: Length of the longest chain with x as maximum element
terms.example: Minimal elements have rank 1; elements covering one minimal element have rank 2
terms.connects: Elements with equal rank form an antichain

given.plan: Apply Mirsky's theorem: partition into n antichains by rank level; the whole poset is their union.

steps.title: Define Mirsky rank layers
steps.formula_raw: min antichains to partition = length of longest chain
steps.apply: Define rank(x) = length of longest chain with x as maximum. Layer k = {x : rank(x) = k} is an antichain: if x <= y and rank(x) = rank(y), the chain through x to y would be longer, contradicting maximality. Ranks run from 1 to n.
steps.note: This creates exactly n distinct antichain layers.

steps.title: State the decomposition as a union
steps.formula_raw: P = Layer_1 union Layer_2 union ... union Layer_n
steps.apply: The n Mirsky layers are disjoint and together cover all elements of P. So the partial order is written as a UNION of n antichains.
steps.note: Dilworth's theorem is the dual: minimum chain cover = size of largest antichain.

solution.result: union (a partial order with longest chain length n is a union of n antichains)

questions.formula_note: mirsky-theorem is key: min antichains to partition a poset = length of longest chain; the blank is filled with 'union'.

questions.question:
The less-than relation, <, on reals is

A. a partial ordering since it is asymmetric and reflexive
B. a partial ordering since it is antisymmetric and reflexive
C. not a partial ordering because it is not asymmetric and not reflexive
D. not a partial ordering because it is not antisymmetric and reflexive
E. none of the above
questions.to_find: Whether < on reals is a partial order and which option correctly characterizes it

understand.plain: A partial order must be reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive. The strict less-than < fails reflexivity: a < a is false for every real a. So < is irreflexive, not reflexive, and is NOT a partial order. Options A and B wrongly claim < is reflexive. Option C wrongly says < is not asymmetric (< IS asymmetric). Option D is also incorrect. Only E correctly identifies that none of A-D accurately describes <.

keywords.explain: Reflexive (aRa), antisymmetric (aRb and bRa => a=b), transitive (aRb and bRc => aRc).
keywords.example: <= on integers satisfies all three; < fails reflexivity.

keywords.explain: a < a is always false; < is irreflexive, the opposite of reflexive.
keywords.example: 3 < 3 is false; no real number is strictly less than itself.

keywords.explain: An irreflexive, asymmetric, transitive relation; distinct from a (non-strict) partial order.
keywords.example: < on R is a strict total order: irreflexive, asymmetric, transitive.

understand.visual_alt: On the real number line, a < a is impossible (irreflexive), so < violates the reflexivity axiom required for a partial order.

given.aim: Determine if < on reals satisfies partial order axioms and identify the correct description.

terms.meaning: Strict less-than; a < b means a is strictly smaller; a < a is always false
aRa holds for every a in the domain

terms.example: < is asymmetric: a < b implies b < a is false
terms.connects: Option C incorrectly claims < is not asymmetric

given.plan: Check reflexivity of < (fails: a < a is false). Check each option A-D for errors. Conclude E.

steps.title: Test reflexivity of <
steps.apply: Reflexivity requires a < a for all real a. But a < a is always FALSE. So < is NOT reflexive and therefore NOT a partial order. Options A and B both claim < is reflexive - both wrong.
steps.note: < is irreflexive (a < a is always false), the opposite of reflexive.

steps.title: Evaluate options C and D
steps.apply: Option C: says < is not asymmetric. But < IS asymmetric (a < b implies b < a is false). So C is wrong. Option D: says < is not antisymmetric and is reflexive. < is irreflexive (not reflexive), so D is also wrong. None of A-D is correct, so E is the answer.
steps.note: < is a strict partial order: irreflexive, asymmetric, transitive; not a non-strict partial order.

questions.formula_note: partial-order-axioms: < fails reflexivity (a < a is never true); all options A-D misstate the properties of <, so E is correct.

questions.question:
Let X = {3, 6, 12, 24, 48}. Let <= be the partial order defined by X <= Y if X divides Y. Number of edges in the Hasse diagram of (X, <=) is

A. 3
B. 4
C. 9
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: Number of edges (covering relations) in the Hasse diagram of divisibility on {3,6,12,24,48}

understand.plain: A Hasse diagram shows only covering relations: an edge x -> y appears only when x divides y AND there is no element z in the set with x divides z and z divides y. The set {3,6,12,24,48} forms a chain 3|6|12|24|48 (each element is double the previous). A chain of 5 elements has exactly 4 consecutive covering pairs: (3,6), (6,12), (12,24), (24,48). All non-consecutive divisibility pairs have an intermediate element.

keywords.explain: Visual representation showing only covering relations (direct predecessors), omitting transitive edges.
keywords.example: For {1,2,4}: edges 1->2 and 2->4 only; 1->4 is omitted since 2 is between.

keywords.explain: x is covered by y if x divides y with no element z in the set strictly between.
keywords.example: 3 is covered by 6 in {3,6,12}: no element z in the set with 3|z|6.

keywords.explain: A set where each element divides the next; a chain of n elements has n-1 cover relations.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox="0 0 200 340" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line x1="100" y1="60" x2="100" y2="100" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="100" y1="120" x2="100" y2="160" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="100" y1="180" x2="100" y2="220" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2.5"/><line x1="100" y1="240" x2="100" y2="280" stroke="#B4B2A9" stroke-width="2.5"/><circle cx="100" cy="40" r="20" class="hi"/><text x="100" y="45" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">48</text><circle cx="100" cy="110" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="115" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">24</text><circle cx="100" cy="170" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="175" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">12</text><circle cx="100" cy="230" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="235" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">6</text><circle cx="100" cy="300" r="20" class="bg"/><text x="100" y="305" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Vertical Hasse diagram chain: 3 at the bottom, then 6, 12, 24, 48 at top, with 4 edges.

given.aim: Count the covering edges in the Hasse diagram of ({3,6,12,24,48}, divisibility).

terms.meaning: Five elements forming a divisibility chain
terms.connects: The poset whose Hasse diagram we draw

terms.meaning: x|y with no z in X satisfying x|z|y
terms.example: (12,24): 12|24 and no z in X lies strictly between
terms.connects: Each covering pair contributes one Hasse edge

terms.meaning: A chain of n elements has n-1 covering pairs

given.plan: Verify all consecutive pairs are covers and all non-consecutive pairs have an intermediate element; count 4 covering edges.

steps.title: Identify all covering pairs
steps.formula_raw: x covers y iff x < y with no z satisfying x < z < y in the poset
steps.apply: (3,6): 3|6, no z in X with 3|z|6 - cover. (6,12): 6|12, no intermediate z - cover. (12,24): 12|24, no intermediate - cover. (24,48): 24|48, no intermediate - cover. Non-consecutive: (3,12) has 6 between; (6,24) has 12; (12,48) has 24; etc. - all non-covers.
steps.note: The set forms a pure chain, so only consecutive pairs are covers.

steps.title: Count edges
steps.apply: Covering pairs: (3,6), (6,12), (12,24), (24,48) = 4 edges. A chain of 5 elements has 5 - 1 = 4 Hasse diagram edges. Answer is B.
steps.note: Options A=3 and C=9 are incorrect; B=4 is correct.

questions.formula_note: hasse-cover-relation is key: only direct divisibility pairs with no intermediate element become Hasse edges; the 5-element chain yields exactly 4 edges.

questions.question:
A partial order <= is defined on the set S = {a_0, a_1, ..., a_n} as a_i <= a_i for all i and a_i <= a_{i+1} for all i, where n >= 1. The number of total orders on the set S which contain the partial order <= is

A. 1
B. n
C. (n+1)!/2
D. (n+1)!
questions.to_find: The number of total orders on S that extend the chain partial order a_0 <= a_1 <= ... <= a_n

understand.plain: The given partial order says a_0 <= a_1 <= ... <= a_n. By transitivity, a_i <= a_j for all i <= j, so every pair of elements is already comparable. This means the partial order is already a total order (a chain). A total order extending a partial order must preserve all existing relations. Since all elements are fixed in the order a_0, a_1, ..., a_n, exactly 1 total order extends it.

keywords.explain: A partial order where every two elements are comparable.
keywords.example: Standard <= on integers is total; subset inclusion is not.

keywords.explain: A total order consistent with all given partial order relations.
keywords.example: A chain of n elements has exactly 1 linear extension; an antichain of n has n! extensions.

keywords.explain: A poset where all elements are mutually comparable; has exactly one linear extension (itself).
keywords.example: a_0 < a_1 < ... < a_n is a chain with 1 linear extension.

understand.visual_alt: Vertical chain a_0 below a_1 below ... below a_n; all elements already comparable, leaving exactly one possible total ordering.

given.aim: Count total orders on {a_0, ..., a_n} consistent with the chain partial order a_0 <= ... <= a_n.

terms.meaning: Set of n+1 elements with defined partial order
terms.connects: Domain of the partial and total orders

terms.meaning: a_i <= a_i (reflexive) and a_i <= a_{i+1} for all valid i; by transitivity a_i <= a_j for i <= j
Number of total orderings compatible with all given relations
terms.example: Full chain: 1; full antichain of n+1 elements: (n+1)!

given.plan: Apply transitivity to show the partial order is already total, then conclude only 1 linear extension exists.

steps.title: Show the partial order is a total chain
steps.formula_raw: a total order extending a chain must be the chain itself
steps.apply: From a_0<=a_1, a_1<=a_2, ..., a_{n-1}<=a_n, apply transitivity: a_i<=a_j for all i<j. Every pair (a_i,a_j) is comparable. The partial order is already a total order.
steps.note: Transitivity closes: a_0<=a_1 and a_1<=a_2 gives a_0<=a_2, and so on.

steps.title: Count linear extensions
steps.apply: Any total order extending this chain must preserve a_i<=a_j for all i<j. The only such ordering is a_0, a_1, ..., a_n. Count = 1.
steps.note: Compare: an antichain of n+1 elements has (n+1)! extensions since any permutation is valid.

questions.formula_note: linear-extension-poset is key: the partial order is already a full chain (all elements comparable by transitivity), so only 1 total order extends it.
